title_en: On Haste to Do Good Deeds (Al-Mubadara ila al-Khayrat)
content_en:
The concluding chapter of this section of Riyadh al-Salihin addresses a matter of practical spiritual urgency: the Islamic imperative to hasten toward good deeds without delay. Al-Nawawi grounds this chapter in the recognition that human life is short, that tomorrow is not guaranteed, and that every moment of delay in doing good is a moment that may never be recovered. The Quran commands: 'Race to forgiveness from your Lord and a garden as wide as the heavens and earth, prepared for the righteous' (Quran 3:133). The Arabic word 'sari'u' — race, hasten — is a command of urgency that leaves no room for procrastination.

The primary hadith of this chapter is a stark prophetic warning: 'Be quick to do good deeds before the coming of trials that are like portions of a dark night — in which a man is a believer in the morning and a disbeliever in the evening, a believer in the evening and a disbeliever in the morning — selling his religion for a portion of the goods of this world.' (Muslim.) This narration, which refers to the great tribulations (fitan) that will afflict the ummah in later times, issues a practical warning: the religious conditions of the future may make good deeds more difficult, religious commitment more socially costly, and moral clarity harder to maintain. The person who acts righteously today, while conditions are still relatively favorable, is wise; the person who delays until they 'feel ready' or 'the time is right' may find that the time for ease has passed.

Al-Nawawi includes the counsel of the Prophet ﷺ to take advantage of five things before five others: 'Your youth before your old age, your health before your illness, your wealth before your poverty, your free time before your preoccupation, and your life before your death.' (Al-Hakim, authenticated by al-Albani.) This famous 'five before five' hadith identifies the specific windows of opportunity that human life provides for good deeds — youth, health, wealth, time, and life itself — and frames each as a trust from Allah that must be invested before it expires. Old age brings fatigue, illness limits capacity, poverty restricts options, preoccupation with responsibilities reduces available time, and death ends all opportunity. The wise believer acts in the window they have, not the window they imagine they will have.

The chapter also includes the story of the man who came to the Prophet ﷺ and asked: 'Which charity is most rewarded?' The Prophet ﷺ replied: 'To give when you are healthy and avaricious, fearing poverty and hoping for wealth. Do not delay until you are on your deathbed and say: Give such-and-such to so-and-so — for at that point it already belongs to the heirs.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) This narration establishes that the reward of charitable giving is tied not only to the amount given but to the spiritual cost to the giver — giving when it is genuinely difficult demonstrates the sincerity of the love for Allah that overcomes the love of wealth.

The spirit of mubadara — hastening toward good — is ultimately the spirit of a person who understands the fundamental Islamic truth that this world is a brief passage and the Hereafter is the permanent abode. Every moment of this passage is an opportunity. Riyadh al-Salihin as a whole is a map of those opportunities — and this final chapter of the opening section calls the reader to begin using that map without delay.
